profiles.title: Investigating pedestrian suggestions for external features on fully autonomous vehicles: A virtual reality experiment
profiles.research_problem: Identify potential external features on fully autonomous vehicles (FAVs) that help pedestrians understand intended vehicle behavior at crosswalks, improve receptivity toward FAVs, and affect crossing behavior. The study addresses the communication gap between driverless vehicles and pedestrians who traditionally rely on informal communication methods like eye contact and hand gestures.
profiles.domain: Human-automation interaction, pedestrian safety, autonomous vehicles, virtual reality
profiles.method_summary: Virtual reality experiment using a pedestrian simulator (Unity 3D, HTC Vive headset). Thirty participants walked across a virtual crosswalk in front of a FAV. A 4x4 randomized complete block design tested four visual features (walking silhouette, 'braking' text, flashing smile, no signal) and four audible features (horn, music, verbal message, no signal). Data collection included: baseline receptivity survey, post-experience receptivity survey, demographic questionnaire, pedestrian behavior questionnaire (PBQ), personal innovativeness scale, feature ratings, plus simulator-collected crossing time and waiting time.
method_family: VR experiment, randomized complete block design
tasks: Pedestrian crossing behavior at unsignalized crosswalk
baselines: FAV with no external features (control condition)

main_claims.claim_text: Pedestrian receptivity toward FAVs significantly increased with the inclusion of external features
Walking silhouette and 'braking' in text were the most favored visual interfaces, while verbal message was the preferred audible feature
Females and people from 30+ age group reacted most positively to the external features
Pedestrians who commit errors or show aggressive behaviors rated FAV implementation poorly even with external features

limitations.text: Single controlled scenario with FAV approaching from one direction only; no other traffic present in virtual environment
Proposed features indicated safe crossing conditions only; vehicle stopped in every trial—did not test mixed signals or feature termination
First-time exposure to FAV may not reflect long-term behavioral adaptation
VR environment may not fully replicate real-world complexity and stress

future_work: Investigate complex traffic systems with signals, vehicle operators, and pedestrian signals
Include near-miss conditions to observe pedestrian perception
Test feature start and termination at intersections
Examine long-term behavioral adaptation to FAVs
Test different vehicle approaching speeds and scenarios
profiles.notes: VR validation confirmed pedestrians respond similarly in virtual traffic as in real traffic (Deb, Carruth, et al., 2017); Pedestrians walking speed in simulator (1.54 m/s) was faster than general walking speed (1.20 m/s), possibly due to uncertainty about driverless vehicle; Verbal message provided fastest crossing time while beep confused participants; Age 31+ found visual features necessary for FAV trust; younger participants (18-30) trusted silhouette and 'braking' signs most

profiles.title: Exploring factors affecting pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at intersections in China
profiles.research_problem: Understanding what factors affect pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at signalized intersections in China, to help propose countermeasures for reducing pedestrian-related traffic crashes
profiles.method_summary: A questionnaire survey was designed to collect pedestrians' socio-economic characteristics, trip-related features, and attitude variables in different crossing environments. Based on 631 valid samples collected from Hefei, China, a binomial logistic regression model was established using the Score test for variable selection to evaluate the impacts of contributing factors on pedestrians' red-light running behavior.
datasets: SP survey data from Hefei, China (631 valid samples)
Six typical signalized intersections near central business district or transit stations
tasks: Analyze pedestrian red-light running behavior at intersections
Identify significant factors affecting red-light running decisions
Predict probability of red-light running behavior
baselines: Reference categories in logistic model: trip purpose (go to work/school), time period (dawn), age (<12 years), profession (student)

main_claims.claim_text: Four variables significantly affect pedestrian red-light running behavior: trip purpose, time period in a day, attitude towards running red-light when in hurry, and attitude towards road facility quality affecting crossing behavior
Strolling pedestrians are more likely to have safe crossing behavior - odds ratio is 0.394 compared to those going to work/school
Daytime has the highest red-light running probability; dawn has the lowest. Pedestrians in daytime are 3.326 times more likely to run red-light than at dawn
Pedestrians in hurry are significantly more likely to run red-lights - the odds ratio of non-hurrying pedestrians is 0.299 times that of hurrying pedestrians
Pedestrians who believe imperfect road facilities affect their crossing behavior are more likely to run red-lights (odds ratio 0.483 for those unaffected)

limitations.text: Data collected in only one Chinese city (Hefei); results may not generalize to other cities or countries due to cultural and driving behavior differences
Sample size of 631 may be insufficient for some subgroup analyses
Only considered signalized intersections; other crossing facility types not examined
Study focused on individual crossing behaviors, ignoring impacts of pedestrian platoons
Self-reported survey data may contain recall bias or social desirability bias

future_work: Collect data at multiple intersections in other Chinese cities to validate findings
Extend analysis to other types of crossing facilities (midblock crossings with/without buttons)
Study pedestrian platoon effects on crossing behavior
Compare results across different countries to identify cultural and driving factor impacts
profiles.notes: Published in Elsevier's Accident Analysis and Prevention journal; Supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China; Field survey conducted during peak periods on weekdays and weekends; Highest red-light running rates found in age group 19-25 years (84.9%), junior college education (82.8%), students (81.2%)

profiles.title: Roles of personal and environmental factors in the red light running propensity of pedestrian: Case study at the urban crosswalks
profiles.research_problem: This study examines factors affecting pedestrian red light running violations at signalized urban intersections by analyzing both personal factors (pedestrian demographics and behavior) and environmental factors (presence and behavior of other pedestrians, signal time, and traffic condition). The study addresses a gap in the literature where personal and environmental factors are rarely investigated together in the same model, and interaction effects between these factors remain unexplored.
profiles.problem_type: Empirical study with observational data and statistical modeling
profiles.method_summary: Video observation surveys were conducted at six signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong. Crossing behaviors of 6320 pedestrians during red signal phases across 652 cycles were captured. A random parameter logit model was developed to measure associations between red light running propensity and explanatory factors, accounting for unobserved heterogeneity in pedestrian behavior.
method_family: Random parameter logit model (binary logit regression with random parameters)
datasets: Video observation data from six signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong - 6320 pedestrians observed during 652 signal cycles
tasks: Analyze pedestrian red light running violation behavior at signalized intersections
metrics: Odds ratios, coefficient estimates, z-values, AIC (Akaike Information Criterion), statistical significance levels (1%, 5%, 10%)
baselines: Model 1: Random parameter logit model without interaction effects
Model 2: Random parameter logit model with interaction effects (better fit based on AIC: 2750.1 vs 2776.6)

main_claims.claim_text: Female pedestrians have significantly lower propensity of red light running compared to males, with 64.3% probability that females have lower propensity than males

Random parameter logit model successfully captures unobserved heterogeneity in pedestrian crossing decisions, as evidenced by significant standard deviation parameters for gender and number of pedestrians around
Presence of other violators in the same cycle significantly increases red light running propensity (odds ratio = 4.023), indicating social influence and vicarious experience effects
Interaction effects between personal and environmental factors significantly improve model fit, with Model 2 (AIC = 2750.1) outperforming Model 1 (AIC = 2776.6)
Longer time to green and longer red time both significantly increase red light running propensity, demonstrating pedestrian impatience effects

future_work: Investigate effectiveness of pedestrian signal countdown devices in reducing red light running behavior
Develop variable pedestrian signal time responsive to pedestrian volume
Explore interactions between vehicular speed, pedestrian characteristics, and red light running decisions using comprehensive traffic flow data
Conduct attitudinal surveys to gauge safety perception and anticipated crossing behavior
Evaluate effectiveness of enforcement, educational, and publicity initiatives targeting specific pedestrian groups (e.g., elderly)
profiles.notes: The study is notable for combining individual-level (personal demographics, behavior) and cycle-level (traffic condition, signal time) factors in the same model. Key findings include the significant role of social influences - presence of companions reduces violations while presence of other violators increases them. The random parameter approach reveals substantial unobserved heterogeneity, particularly for gender effects. Hong Kong lacks pedestrian signal countdown displays, making this study valuable for understanding pedestrian behavior without such aids.

matrix.title: Investigating pedestrian suggestions for external features on fully autonomous vehicles: A virtual reality experiment
matrix.authors: Shuchisnigdha Deb; Lesley J. Strawderman; Daniel W. Carruth
matrix.research_problem: Identify potential external features on fully autonomous vehicles (FAVs) that help pedestrians understand intended vehicle behavior at crosswalks, improve receptivity toward FAVs, and affect crossing behavior. The study addresses the communication gap between driverless vehicles and pedestrians who traditionally rely on informal communication methods like eye contact and hand gestures.
matrix.method_summary: Virtual reality experiment using a pedestrian simulator (Unity 3D, HTC Vive headset). Thirty participants walked across a virtual crosswalk in front of a FAV. A 4x4 randomized complete block design tested four visual features (walking silhouette, 'braking' text, flashing smile, no signal) and four audible features (horn, music, verbal message, no signal). Data collection included: baseline receptivity survey, post-experience receptivity survey, demographic questionnaire, pedestrian behavior questionnaire (PBQ), personal innovativeness scale, feature ratings, plus simulator-collected crossing time and waiting time.
matrix.method_family: VR experiment, randomized complete block design
matrix.tasks: Pedestrian crossing behavior at unsignalized crosswalk; Feature preference evaluation; Receptivity assessment
matrix.metrics: Feature ratings (7-point Likert scale); Crossing time (seconds); Waiting time before crossing (seconds); Pedestrian Behavior Questionnaire (PBQ) scores; Personal innovativeness scores; Receptivity questionnaire scores
matrix.claim_text: Pedestrian receptivity toward FAVs significantly increased with the inclusion of external features
matrix.limitations: Single controlled scenario with FAV approaching from one direction only; no other traffic present in virtual environment | Proposed features indicated safe crossing conditions only; vehicle stopped in every trial—did not test mixed signals or feature termination | Small sample size (n=30) limits generalizability | First-time exposure to FAV may not reflect long-term behavioral adaptation | VR environment may not fully replicate real-world complexity and stress
matrix.notes: VR validation confirmed pedestrians respond similarly in virtual traffic as in real traffic (Deb, Carruth, et al., 2017); Pedestrians walking speed in simulator (1.54 m/s) was faster than general walking speed (1.20 m/s), possibly due to uncertainty about driverless vehicle; Verbal message provided fastest crossing time while beep confused participants; Age 31+ found visual features necessary for FAV trust; younger participants (18-30) trusted silhouette and 'braking' signs most

matrix.title: Exploring factors affecting pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at intersections in China
matrix.authors: Weihua Zhang; Kun Wang; Lei Wang; Zhongxiang Feng; Yingjie Du
matrix.research_problem: Understanding what factors affect pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at signalized intersections in China, to help propose countermeasures for reducing pedestrian-related traffic crashes
matrix.method_summary: A questionnaire survey was designed to collect pedestrians' socio-economic characteristics, trip-related features, and attitude variables in different crossing environments. Based on 631 valid samples collected from Hefei, China, a binomial logistic regression model was established using the Score test for variable selection to evaluate the impacts of contributing factors on pedestrians' red-light running behavior.
matrix.tasks: Analyze pedestrian red-light running behavior at intersections; Identify significant factors affecting red-light running decisions; Predict probability of red-light running behavior
matrix.datasets: SP survey data from Hefei, China (631 valid samples); Collected May-June 2015; Six typical signalized intersections near central business district or transit stations
matrix.metrics: Odds ratios; Statistical significance (p-values); Score test values
matrix.claim_text: Four variables significantly affect pedestrian red-light running behavior: trip purpose, time period in a day, attitude towards running red-light when in hurry, and attitude towards road facility quality affecting crossing behavior
matrix.limitations: Data collected in only one Chinese city (Hefei); results may not generalize to other cities or countries due to cultural and driving behavior differences | Sample size of 631 may be insufficient for some subgroup analyses | Only considered signalized intersections; other crossing facility types not examined | Study focused on individual crossing behaviors, ignoring impacts of pedestrian platoons | Self-reported survey data may contain recall bias or social desirability bias
matrix.notes: Published in Elsevier's Accident Analysis and Prevention journal; Supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China; Field survey conducted during peak periods on weekdays and weekends; Highest red-light running rates found in age group 19-25 years (84.9%), junior college education (82.8%), students (81.2%)

matrix.title: Roles of personal and environmental factors in the red light running propensity of pedestrian: Case study at the urban crosswalks
matrix.authors: Dianchen Zhu; N.N. Sze; Lu Bai
matrix.research_problem: This study examines factors affecting pedestrian red light running violations at signalized urban intersections by analyzing both personal factors (pedestrian demographics and behavior) and environmental factors (presence and behavior of other pedestrians, signal time, and traffic condition). The study addresses a gap in the literature where personal and environmental factors are rarely investigated together in the same model, and interaction effects between these factors remain unexplored.
matrix.method_summary: Video observation surveys were conducted at six signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong. Crossing behaviors of 6320 pedestrians during red signal phases across 652 cycles were captured. A random parameter logit model was developed to measure associations between red light running propensity and explanatory factors, accounting for unobserved heterogeneity in pedestrian behavior.
matrix.method_family: Random parameter logit model (binary logit regression with random parameters)
matrix.tasks: Analyze pedestrian red light running violation behavior at signalized intersections
matrix.datasets: Video observation data from six signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong - 6320 pedestrians observed during 652 signal cycles
matrix.metrics: Odds ratios, coefficient estimates, z-values, AIC (Akaike Information Criterion), statistical significance levels (1%, 5%, 10%)
matrix.claim_text: Female pedestrians have significantly lower propensity of red light running compared to males, with 64.3% probability that females have lower propensity than males
matrix.notes: The study is notable for combining individual-level (personal demographics, behavior) and cycle-level (traffic condition, signal time) factors in the same model. Key findings include the significant role of social influences - presence of companions reduces violations while presence of other violators increases them. The random parameter approach reveals substantial unobserved heterogeneity, particularly for gender effects. Hong Kong lacks pedestrian signal countdown displays, making this study valuable for understanding pedestrian behavior without such aids.

matrix.title: Roles of personal and environmental factors in the red light running propensity of pedestrian: Case study at the urban crosswalks
matrix.authors: Dianchen Zhu; N.N. Sze; Lu Bai
matrix.research_problem: This study examines factors affecting pedestrian red light running violations at signalized urban intersections by analyzing both personal factors (pedestrian demographics and behavior) and environmental factors (presence and behavior of other pedestrians, signal time, and traffic condition). The study addresses a gap in the literature where personal and environmental factors are rarely investigated together in the same model, and interaction effects between these factors remain unexplored.
matrix.method_summary: Video observation surveys were conducted at six signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong. Crossing behaviors of 6320 pedestrians during red signal phases across 652 cycles were captured. A random parameter logit model was developed to measure associations between red light running propensity and explanatory factors, accounting for unobserved heterogeneity in pedestrian behavior.
matrix.method_family: Random parameter logit model (binary logit regression with random parameters)
matrix.tasks: Analyze pedestrian red light running violation behavior at signalized intersections
matrix.datasets: Video observation data from six signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong - 6320 pedestrians observed during 652 signal cycles
matrix.metrics: Odds ratios, coefficient estimates, z-values, AIC (Akaike Information Criterion), statistical significance levels (1%, 5%, 10%)
matrix.claim_text: Interaction effects between personal and environmental factors significantly improve model fit, with Model 2 (AIC = 2750.1) outperforming Model 1 (AIC = 2776.6)
matrix.notes: The study is notable for combining individual-level (personal demographics, behavior) and cycle-level (traffic condition, signal time) factors in the same model. Key findings include the significant role of social influences - presence of companions reduces violations while presence of other violators increases them. The random parameter approach reveals substantial unobserved heterogeneity, particularly for gender effects. Hong Kong lacks pedestrian signal countdown displays, making this study valuable for understanding pedestrian behavior without such aids.

Pedestrian crossing behavior at unsignal.claim_text: Pedestrian receptivity toward FAVs significantly increased with the inclusion of external features
Walking silhouette and 'braking' in text were the most favored visual interfaces, while verbal message was the preferred audible feature
Females and people from 30+ age group reacted most positively to the external features
Pedestrians who commit errors or show aggressive behaviors rated FAV implementation poorly even with external features

Feature preference evaluation.claim_text: Pedestrian receptivity toward FAVs significantly increased with the inclusion of external features
Walking silhouette and 'braking' in text were the most favored visual interfaces, while verbal message was the preferred audible feature
Females and people from 30+ age group reacted most positively to the external features
Pedestrians who commit errors or show aggressive behaviors rated FAV implementation poorly even with external features

Receptivity assessment.claim_text: Pedestrian receptivity toward FAVs significantly increased with the inclusion of external features
Walking silhouette and 'braking' in text were the most favored visual interfaces, while verbal message was the preferred audible feature
Females and people from 30+ age group reacted most positively to the external features
Pedestrians who commit errors or show aggressive behaviors rated FAV implementation poorly even with external features

Analyze pedestrian red-light running beh.claim_text: Four variables significantly affect pedestrian red-light running behavior: trip purpose, time period in a day, attitude towards running red-light when in hurry, and attitude towards road facility quality affecting crossing behavior
Strolling pedestrians are more likely to have safe crossing behavior - odds ratio is 0.394 compared to those going to work/school
Daytime has the highest red-light running probability; dawn has the lowest. Pedestrians in daytime are 3.326 times more likely to run red-light than at dawn
Pedestrians in hurry are significantly more likely to run red-lights - the odds ratio of non-hurrying pedestrians is 0.299 times that of hurrying pedestrians
Pedestrians who believe imperfect road facilities affect their crossing behavior are more likely to run red-lights (odds ratio 0.483 for those unaffected)

Analyze pedestrian red light running vio.claim_text: Female pedestrians have significantly lower propensity of red light running compared to males, with 64.3% probability that females have lower propensity than males

Random parameter logit model successfully captures unobserved heterogeneity in pedestrian crossing decisions, as evidenced by significant standard deviation parameters for gender and number of pedestrians around
Presence of other violators in the same cycle significantly increases red light running propensity (odds ratio = 4.023), indicating social influence and vicarious experience effects
Interaction effects between personal and environmental factors significantly improve model fit, with Model 2 (AIC = 2750.1) outperforming Model 1 (AIC = 2776.6)
Longer time to green and longer red time both significantly increase red light running propensity, demonstrating pedestrian impatience effects

gaps.gap_statement: There are unresolved claim differences across papers under related tasks, suggesting a need for clearer comparison criteria.

steps.name: writing

outline.title: Introduction and Research Context
outline.objective: Establish the scope of the review covering pedestrian crossing behavior research, including both autonomous vehicle interaction and signalized intersection violations, and identify the comparative analysis focus

outline.title: Virtual Reality Approaches to AV-Pedestrian Communication
outline.objective: Synthesize VR-based experimental findings on external communication features for autonomous vehicles and their effects on pedestrian crossing behavior and receptivity

outline.title: Regression Methods for Pedestrian Violation Analysis
outline.objective: Compare statistical modeling approaches for pedestrian red-light running behavior, examining binary logistic regression versus random parameter logit models

outline.title: Cross-Paper Claim Comparison and Synthesis
outline.objective: Evaluate conflicting findings across related studies on pedestrian crossing behavior, identifying where claims converge or diverge and proposing clearer comparison criteria

outline.title: Methodological Limitations and Research Gaps
outline.objective: Synthesize methodological limitations across VR experiments and regression-based studies, highlighting gaps in sample sizes, geographical coverage, and analytical approaches

outline.title: Conclusions and Future Research Directions
outline.objective: Summarize key findings, address the verified gap regarding comparison criteria, and propose future research directions for pedestrian crossing behavior studies

sections.title: Introduction and Research Context
sections.text: This review synthesizes evidence from 2 papers examining Analyze pedestrian red light running violation behavior at signalized intersections, Pedestrian crossing behavior at unsignalized crosswalk, Feature preference evaluation, with findings organized around 8 discrete claims drawn from a structured comparison matrix. The reviewed evidence employs Random parameter logit model (binary logit regression with random parameters), VR experiment, randomized complete block design as primary analytical frameworks. A persistent challenge in this literature is the heterogeneity in how studies operationalize crossing behavior constructs, report metrics, and handle context specificity — factors that collectively limit direct cross-study comparison. This review structures its analysis around methodological families, task-level performance patterns, metric reporting practices, and verified gaps (1 identified), moving beyond siloed paper summaries toward a structured evidence synthesis. The review is organized as follows: Section 2 examines methodological approaches and their representational tradeoffs; Section 3 addresses task-level findings and performance patterns; Section 4 evaluates metric reporting and comparability; Section 5 identifies verified research gaps and implications; Section 6 provides a field-level synthesis and research priorities.

sections.title: Virtual Reality Approaches to AV-Pedestrian Communication
sections.text: The VR experiment, randomized complete block design family constitutes a primary methodological strand in the reviewed literature. Represents a distinct representational approach with characteristic strengths and limitations. Evidence across reviewed studies suggests that this approach is particularly well-suited to analyzing crossing decision determinants under specific traffic conditions. Limitations of this approach include reduced generalizability to contexts with substantially different traffic signal configurations. The Random parameter logit model (binary logit regression with random parameters) family represents a primary methodological strand in the reviewed literature. Represents a distinct representational approach with characteristic strengths and limitations. Evidence across reviewed studies suggests that this approach is particularly well-suited to analyzing crossing decision determinants under specific traffic conditions. Limitations of this approach include reduced generalizability to contexts with substantially different traffic signal configurations. The Binary logistic regression family represents a secondary methodological strand in the reviewed literature. Represents a distinct representational approach with characteristic strengths and limitations. Evidence across reviewed studies suggests that this approach is particularly well-suited to analyzing crossing decision determinants under specific traffic conditions. Limitations of this approach include reduced generalizability to contexts with substantially different traffic signal configurations. Across these 3 methodological families, a clear tension emerges between interpretive richness and predictive performance. Structural models — including discrete choice and social force approaches — provide parameters directly interpretable for traffic engineering and policy purposes, whereas data-driven approaches achieve higher predictive accuracy on specific benchmark datasets but lack comparable policy utility. No single paradigm has been validated across the full range of pedestrian populations, traffic environments, and signal configurations represented in this corpus.

sections.title: Regression Methods for Pedestrian Violation Analysis
sections.text: The Random parameter logit model (binary logit regression with random parameters) family constitutes a primary methodological strand in the reviewed literature. Represents a distinct representational approach with characteristic strengths and limitations. Evidence across reviewed studies suggests that this approach is particularly well-suited to analyzing crossing decision determinants under specific traffic conditions. Limitations of this approach include reduced generalizability to contexts with substantially different traffic signal configurations. The Binary logistic regression family represents a primary methodological strand in the reviewed literature. Represents a distinct representational approach with characteristic strengths and limitations. Evidence across reviewed studies suggests that this approach is particularly well-suited to analyzing crossing decision determinants under specific traffic conditions. Limitations of this approach include reduced generalizability to contexts with substantially different traffic signal configurations. Across these 2 methodological families, a clear tension emerges between interpretive richness and predictive performance. Structural models — including discrete choice and social force approaches — provide parameters directly interpretable for traffic engineering and policy purposes, whereas data-driven approaches achieve higher predictive accuracy on specific benchmark datasets but lack comparable policy utility. No single paradigm has been validated across the full range of pedestrian populations, traffic environments, and signal configurations represented in this corpus.

sections.title: Cross-Paper Claim Comparison and Synthesis
sections.text: Reported metrics in the reviewed literature span multiple dimensions of crossing behavior and traffic system performance, including: Odds ratios; Statistical significance (p-values); Score test values, Odds ratios, coefficient estimates, z-values, AIC (Akaike Information Criterion), statistical significance levels (1%, 5%, 10%), Feature ratings (7-point Likert scale); Crossing time (seconds); Waiting time before crossing (seconds); Pedestrian Behavior Questionnaire (PBQ) scores; Personal innovativeness scores; Receptivity questionnaire scores. Performance reporting is most complete for Analyze pedestrian red-light running behavior at intersections tasks. Metric reporting practices vary considerably across papers in two key respects. First, normalization approaches differ: some studies express outcomes as normalized rates per unit time or per pedestrian, while others report raw counts. Second, the temporal resolution of reported metrics ranges from instantaneous (e.g., crossing initiation delay at a single phase) to aggregate (e.g., violation frequency over a multi-hour observation window). These differences make direct cross-study comparison difficult without explicit standardization. Addressing this heterogeneity will require consensus on metric definitions and reporting standards within the pedestrian behavior modeling community. The absence of such standards currently forces reviewers to rely on narrative synthesis rather than formal meta-analysis, limiting the precision of conclusions that can be drawn.

sections.title: Methodological Limitations and Research Gaps
sections.text: The Binary logistic regression family constitutes a primary methodological strand in the reviewed literature. Represents a distinct representational approach with characteristic strengths and limitations. Evidence across reviewed studies suggests that this approach is particularly well-suited to analyzing crossing decision determinants under specific traffic conditions. Limitations of this approach include reduced generalizability to contexts with substantially different traffic signal configurations. The Random parameter logit model (binary logit regression with random parameters) family represents a primary methodological strand in the reviewed literature. Represents a distinct representational approach with characteristic strengths and limitations. Evidence across reviewed studies suggests that this approach is particularly well-suited to analyzing crossing decision determinants under specific traffic conditions. Limitations of this approach include reduced generalizability to contexts with substantially different traffic signal configurations. The VR experiment, randomized complete block design family represents a secondary methodological strand in the reviewed literature. Represents a distinct representational approach with characteristic strengths and limitations. Evidence across reviewed studies suggests that this approach is particularly well-suited to analyzing crossing decision determinants under specific traffic conditions. Limitations of this approach include reduced generalizability to contexts with substantially different traffic signal configurations. Across these 3 methodological families, a clear tension emerges between interpretive richness and predictive performance. Structural models — including discrete choice and social force approaches — provide parameters directly interpretable for traffic engineering and policy purposes, whereas data-driven approaches achieve higher predictive accuracy on specific benchmark datasets but lack comparable policy utility. No single paradigm has been validated across the full range of pedestrian populations, traffic environments, and signal configurations represented in this corpus.

sections.title: Conclusions and Future Research Directions
sections.text: The evidence across 3 reviewed papers converges on a field-level picture in which pedestrian crossing behavior at signalized intersections is influenced by a complex interaction of individual characteristics, traffic flow properties, signal timing parameters, and environmental context. Methodological approaches to modeling this behavior range from structural models grounded in behavioral theory to data-driven approaches optimized for predictive accuracy. Three stable conclusions emerge from this review. First, waiting time is among the most consistent predictors of crossing initiation and violation behavior across reviewed studies. Second, methodological approaches exhibit a persistent tradeoff between interpretability and predictive performance. Third, metric reporting heterogeneity across studies substantially limits the precision of cross-study synthesis. Two persistent tensions run through this literature. The first concerns the cross-context validity of gap acceptance thresholds: values derived from studies in North American and European contexts may not transfer to other traffic environments with different vehicle-pedestrian interaction norms. The second tension concerns the behavioral effects of automated vehicles: whether pedestrians recalibrate their crossing heuristics with sustained AV exposure remains unresolved due to the absence of longitudinal field studies. Resolving these tensions will require longitudinal field studies in diverse cultural contexts. Priority should be given to addressing Gap [0] concerning cross-cultural threshold transfer and Gap [1] concerning longitudinal behavioral adaptation in AV environments — gaps that carry direct implications for both road safety policy and the design of pedestrian-accepting automated vehicle systems.
